intro: Calculate the minimum hourly rate you need to charge as a freelancer or consultant to hit your income goal. Account for vacation, non-billable admin time, and working hours to get a realistic number you can actually charge.
howItWorks: Billable Hours = (52 − Vacation Weeks) × Hours per Week × (1 − Non-Billable%). Hourly Rate = Desired Annual Income ÷ Billable Hours. Non-billable time covers admin, marketing, sales calls, accounting, and professional development. Most freelancers spend 20–40% of their time on non-billable activities. Ignoring this is the most common pricing mistake.
interpretationGuide: The resulting hourly rate is your floor — the minimum to hit your income goal. Your actual rate should be higher to account for taxes (30–40% of gross for the self-employed), business expenses, equipment, insurance, and savings. Many freelancers multiply the calculated floor by 1.5–2× to arrive at their market rate. Compare your rate against market benchmarks for your field.

faqs.question: Why should I account for non-billable time?
faqs.answer: As a freelancer, you can't bill for every hour you work. Sales calls, proposals, admin, email, accounting, and professional development all take time. If you bill 40 hours a week but actually work 50, 20% of your time is non-billable. Ignoring this is the #1 cause of freelance underearning.

faqs.question: What percentage of time is typically non-billable?
faqs.answer: Most freelancers report 20–40% non-billable time. A solo consultant with a strong client pipeline might be at 15%. Someone actively growing their business through content and networking might be at 40%. Be honest — track your hours for a week to calibrate.

faqs.question: Should my rate include taxes?
faqs.answer: This calculator gives you a gross rate. You should set aside 25–35% of gross income for income tax and national insurance/self-employment tax. Either increase your target income by this amount, or add a separate tax buffer to the calculated rate.

faqs.question: How many vacation weeks should I include?
faqs.answer: Most freelancers take 4–8 weeks off per year when accounting for public holidays, sick days, and actual vacation. Many new freelancers forget this and end up burned out. Treat yourself at least as well as an employer would treat an employee — factor in proper time off.

faqs.question: What if my calculated rate seems too high for the market?
faqs.answer: Three options: (1) Reduce your income target — are your expenses really as high as assumed? (2) Reduce non-billable time — streamline admin, automate invoicing, use proposal templates. (3) Productise or raise your value — if the market rate is genuinely lower, you'll need to specialise and command premium rates, or go full-time employment instead.

faqs.question: How does the daily rate relate to the hourly rate?
faqs.answer: Daily Rate = Hourly Rate × Hours per Day. This calculator uses your hours-per-week divided by 5 to estimate a standard working day. Adjust this if you work more or fewer hours per day.

faqs.question: Should I charge the same rate to all clients?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Many freelancers charge different rates based on project type, client size, urgency, or whether the work is exciting. The calculator gives your minimum rate — you can charge more for high-value, rush, or specialized work.

faqs.question: What about freelance platform fees?
faqs.answer: If you work through platforms (Upwork, Fiverr, etc.), add their fee percentage to your rate. Upwork takes 10–20%, Fiverr 20%. Factor this as an additional cost on top of your calculated rate.

faqs.question: How do I handle retainers vs one-off projects?
faqs.answer: For retainers, use your hourly rate and estimate hours per month. For one-off projects, multiply your hourly rate by estimated hours and add a project management buffer (10–20%). Always scope clearly and agree on revision limits.

faqs.question: Should I charge by the hour or by the project?
faqs.answer: Project-based pricing is usually better for experienced freelancers — you're paid for value, not time. Hourly rates are better when scope is unclear. Use this calculator to establish your floor, then convert to project pricing by estimating hours and adding a buffer.

faqs.question: How do I handle rate increases with existing clients?
faqs.answer: Give existing clients 30–60 days notice of a rate increase. Frame it around increased expertise, cost of living, or market rates — not personal need. A 10–15% annual increase is reasonable. Losing a client over a rate increase is usually better than being underpaid for years.

faqs.question: What should I include in my income target?
faqs.answer: Desired take-home income + estimated taxes + business expenses (software, equipment, insurance, accounting) + pension/retirement contributions + an emergency fund contribution. Many freelancers target 130–160% of their desired net income as their gross income goal.
